topics.title: Java Operators
topics.description: Master all Java operator categories: arithmetic (+,-,*,/,%), bitwise (&,|,^,~,<<,>>,>>>), relational (==,!=,<,>,<=,>=), logical (&&,||,!), assignment (=,+=,-=,...), ternary (?:), instanceof, and special operators. Understand operator precedence, short-circuit evaluation, and bitwise tricks.
topics.story: Operators are the verbs of Java — they DO things to values. Arithmetic operators are like kitchen tools: + adds ingredients, / divides portions. Bitwise operators work at the atomic level — manipulating individual 1s and 0s like a jeweler setting individual stones. Short-circuit operators (&&, ||) are like lazy evaluators who stop reading once they know the answer: 'if false && (anything)' — why check anything? Logical precedence is like PEMDAS in math, but with 15 levels instead of 4.
topics.analogy: Operator precedence = recipe steps. You must multiply before adding: 2 + 3 * 4 = 14 (not 20). Bitwise & = AND gate circuit — both switches must be ON. | = OR gate — either switch is enough. ^ = XOR gate — exactly one switch (toggles). Short-circuit && = electricity — if the first switch cuts power, the second switch is never reached.
topics.memoryAnchor: Precedence mnemonic (high to low): 'Uncle Percy Makes Really Good Apple Pie, Stewed Occasionally': Unary, Post/Pre-increment, Multiplicative (*/%)), Relational (<>), Bitwise-AND, Bitwise-OR/XOR, Logical-AND(&&), Logical-OR(||), Ternary(?:). Assignment is always last.
topics.firstPrinciples: Why bitwise operators? Modern CPUs execute AND/OR/XOR/SHIFT in single clock cycles — the fastest possible operations. Checking if a number is even: (n & 1) == 0 is one CPU instruction vs n % 2 == 0 which involves division. Bit manipulation is the foundation of compression, encryption, hashing, permissions, and protocols. The entire internet runs on bitwise operations.
topics.bruteForce: Without bitwise: store permissions as array of booleans [canRead, canWrite, canExecute]. Check: array[0]. Toggle: array[0] = !array[0]. Combine: merge arrays. With bitwise: permissions = 0b111. Check read: (perms & 4) != 0. Toggle: perms ^= 4. Combine: permsA | permsB. One integer replaces an entire array.
topics.optimizedApproach: Bitwise operators for performance-critical paths: n & (n-1) clears the lowest set bit, n & -n isolates the lowest set bit, n << 1 doubles (faster than *2), n >> 1 halves (faster than /2). These one-instruction tricks are used in hash tables, Fenwick trees, and bit manipulation problems extensively in competitive programming and system code.
topics.whenToUse: Arithmetic: standard math. Bitwise: permissions/flags, hash functions, low-level protocols, power-of-2 checks, counting set bits (popcount). Shift: fast multiplication/division by powers of 2. Short-circuit (&&/||): guard null checks (obj != null && obj.method()), lazy evaluation. Ternary: simple conditional assignment (not deeply nested). instanceof: type checking before casting (prefer pattern matching in Java 16+).

examples.title: Arithmetic Operators and Integer Division
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":7,"lineCode":"a / b","variables":{"a":17,"b":5,"result":3},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Integer division: 17/5 = 3 (not 3.4). Fractional part DISCARDED — this is truncation toward zero"},{"line":12,"lineCode":"-7 / 2","variables":{"result":-3},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Truncation toward zero: -7/2 = -3.5, truncated to -3. NOT -4 (which would be floor division)"},{"line":13,"lineCode":"-7 % 2","variables":{"result":-1},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Java modulo sign follows dividend: -7%2 = -1 because -7 = (-3)*2 + (-1). Useful mnemonic: remainder has same sign as left operand"},{"line":21,"lineCode":"System.out.println(x++);","variables":{"x":5,"printed":5},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Post-increment: returns current value (5) THEN increments x to 6"},{"line":23,"lineCode":"System.out.println(++x);","variables":{"x":7,"printed":7},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Pre-increment: increments x to 7 FIRST, then returns 7"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[a / b] --> B{types?}
    B -->|both int| C[integer division: truncates toward 0]
    B -->|either double/float| D[floating point division: exact]
    
    E[a % b] --> F[remainder: sign follows a]
    
    G[x++] --> H[return x, then x = x+1]
    I[++x] --> J[x = x+1, then return x]
examples.pseudocode:
Integer division:
  7 / 2 = 3   (truncate, not round)
  -7 / 2 = -3 (truncate toward zero, not floor)

Modulo rule:
  a % b: sign matches a (dividend)
  -7 % 2 = -1  (not +1)

Post-increment: evaluate, then increment
Pre-increment:  increment, then evaluate
examples.explanation: The most common integer arithmetic bug: integer division truncates. 5/2 = 2, not 2.5. To get float division: use 5.0/2 or (double)5/2. The modulo with negative numbers surprises developers: Python uses floor division (-7 % 2 = 1 in Python), Java uses truncated division (-7 % 2 = -1). For mathematical modulo that's always non-negative: ((n % m) + m) % m.
examples.realWorldUse: Pagination: `page = (index / pageSize) + 1`, `offset = (page-1) * pageSize`. Integer division makes this exact. Ring buffer index: `next = (current + 1) % bufferSize` — modulo wraps cleanly. UUID generation uses bitwise ops and shifts extensively. Network checksum algorithms (CRC) are essentially XOR and shift operations.

examples.title: Bitwise Operators — Flags and Permissions
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":9,"lineCode":"0b1010 & 0b1100","variables":{"result":8},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"AND: 1010 & 1100 = 1000 = 8. Only bits where BOTH are 1 survive. Mask: only keeps bits that are 1 in the mask"},{"line":12,"lineCode":"0b1010 | 0b1100","variables":{"result":14},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"OR: 1010 | 1100 = 1110 = 14. Bits where EITHER is 1. Used to SET bits"},{"line":15,"lineCode":"0b1010 ^ 0b1100","variables":{"result":6},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"XOR: 1010 ^ 1100 = 0110 = 6. Bits where EXACTLY ONE is 1. Used to TOGGLE bits"},{"line":20,"lineCode":"int perms = READ | WRITE;","variables":{"perms":6},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"100 | 010 = 110 = 6. Both READ and WRITE bits are set"},{"line":21,"lineCode":"(perms & READ) != 0","variables":{"result":true},"phase":"CONDITION_TRUE","annotation":"110 & 100 = 100 != 0 → READ is set"},{"line":27,"lineCode":"perms &= ~WRITE;","variables":{"perms":5},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"~WRITE = ~010 = ...11111101 (all bits flipped). perms & ~WRITE clears the WRITE bit: 111 & 101 = 101 = 5"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD subgraph Bitwise Operations A[AND &] --> A1[Both bits 1 → 1
Used: check bit, mask] B[OR |] --> B1[Either bit 1 → 1
Used: set bit] C[XOR ^] --> C1[Exactly one 1 → 1
Used: toggle bit] D[NOT ~] --> D1[Flip all bits
Used: create mask for clear] end subgraph Permission Pattern E[Set bit: perms |= FLAG] F[Clear bit: perms &= ~FLAG] G[Check bit: (perms & FLAG) != 0] H[Toggle bit: perms ^= FLAG] end
examples.pseudocode:
SET   bit: value |= mask        (turn on)
CLEAR bit: value &= ~mask       (turn off)
TOGGLE bit: value ^= mask       (flip)
CHECK bit: (value & mask) != 0  (is it on?)

Practical:
  READ=4, WRITE=2, EXEC=1 (Unix rwx = 421)
  chmod 755 = 111 101 101 = 0755 octal
examples.explanation: Bitwise operators are the foundation of all computer operations. File permissions, network masks (IP AND subnet mask = network address), color channels (red = (color >> 16) & 0xFF), hash functions, encryption — all use bitwise ops. XOR has a special property: a ^ b ^ b = a (XOR with same value twice cancels out). This is used in swapping without a temp variable and in parity checks.
examples.realWorldUse: Java's EnumSet uses a long bitmask internally for up to 64 enum values — Set operations (add, remove, contains, union) become single bitwise instructions. Spring Security's GrantedAuthority stores permissions as bitmasks for O(1) permission checks. JPEG encoding uses bitwise shifts for DCT coefficient packing.

examples.title: Shift Operators — Multiply/Divide by Powers of 2
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":5,"lineCode":"x << 1","variables":{"x":1,"result":2},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Left shift by 1: 0001 → 0010 = 2. Equivalent to multiplying by 2. One CPU instruction vs multiplication"},{"line":11,"lineCode":"y >> 1","variables":{"y":100,"result":50},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Arithmetic right shift: 100 = 0110_0100 → 0011_0010 = 50. Sign bit (0) is copied into vacated left bits"},{"line":15,"lineCode":"neg >> 1","variables":{"neg":-100,"result":-50},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"-100 in two's complement: 1111...1001_1100. >> 1: sign bit (1) fills left: 1111...1100_1110 = -50"},{"line":20,"lineCode":"-1 >>> 1","variables":{"result":2147483647},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Unsigned shift: -1 = 1111...1111. >>> 1: zero fills left: 0111...1111 = MAX_VALUE = 2147483647"},{"line":24,"lineCode":"(argb >> 24) & 0xFF","variables":{"argb":"0xFFAABBCC","result":255},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Shift right 24 bits brings alpha byte to position 0-7. & 0xFF masks to 8 bits, removing sign extension"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    subgraph Left Shift
      A[n << k] -->|same as| B[n * 2^k]
      B --> C[Vacated right bits: filled with 0]
    end
    
    subgraph Arithmetic Right Shift
      D[n >> k] -->|same as| E[n / 2^k truncated]
      E --> F[Vacated left bits: filled with sign bit]
    end
    
    subgraph Unsigned Right Shift
      G[n >>> k] --> H[Vacated left bits: filled with 0]
      H --> I[Works correctly for unsigned values]
    end
examples.pseudocode:
<< left shift: multiply by 2^k (fast)
>> right shift: divide by 2^k, preserve sign
>>> unsigned right shift: divide by 2^k, zero fill

Color channel extraction:
  int argb = 0xFFAABBCC;
  red   = (argb >> 16) & 0xFF;  // byte 2
  green = (argb >>  8) & 0xFF;  // byte 1
  blue  =  argb        & 0xFF;  // byte 0

Power of 2 check:
  (n & (n-1)) == 0  // n=8: 1000 & 0111 = 0
examples.explanation: The >>> (unsigned right shift) exists specifically for treating an int as an unsigned 32-bit value. Classic use: int mid = (low + high) >>> 1 — this is the CORRECT way to find the middle of two indices without overflow (low + high might overflow if both are large). Java's Arrays.binarySearch uses this exact idiom. The >> version would work for positive values but fail if the sum overflows to negative.
examples.realWorldUse: Java's HashMap uses bit shifting extensively: hash(key) ^ (hash >>> 16) spreads high bits into low bits to reduce collisions. ArrayList's grow(): int newCapacity = oldCapacity + (oldCapacity >> 1) — grow by 50% with a single shift. Color manipulation in image processing (Android, JavaFX): pack/unpack ARGB values with shifts and masks.

examples.title: Short-Circuit Evaluation and Logical Operators
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":12,"lineCode":"boolean r1 = check(\"A\", false) && check(\"B\", true);","variables":{"callCount":1,"r1":false},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"&& short-circuits: check(A) returns false → entire expression is false. check(B) NEVER CALLED. callCount=1"},{"line":18,"lineCode":"boolean r2 = check(\"A\", true) || check(\"B\", false);","variables":{"callCount":1,"r2":true},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"|| short-circuits: check(A) returns true → entire expression is true. check(B) NEVER CALLED. callCount=1"},{"line":24,"lineCode":"boolean r3 = check(\"A\", false) & check(\"B\", true);","variables":{"callCount":2,"r3":false},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Single & is bitwise AND (not logical). Both sides ALWAYS evaluated. callCount=2"},{"line":29,"lineCode":"s != null && !s.isEmpty()","variables":{"s":null,"result":false},"phase":"CONDITION_FALSE","annotation":"Short-circuit saves us: s==null is false → stop. If we used &, !s.isEmpty() would throw NPE"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    A[a && b] -->|a is false| B[Return false\nb NOT evaluated]
    A -->|a is true| C[Evaluate b, return b]
    
    D[a || b] -->|a is true| E[Return true\nb NOT evaluated]
    D -->|a is false| F[Evaluate b, return b]
    
    G[a & b] --> H[ALWAYS evaluate both sides]
    I[a | b] --> H
examples.pseudocode:
&& (short-circuit AND):
  if left == false: return false immediately
  else: evaluate and return right

|| (short-circuit OR):
  if left == true: return true immediately
  else: evaluate and return right

& and | (bitwise/non-short-circuit):
  ALWAYS evaluate both sides
  Use when right side has desired side effects

Null guard pattern:
  obj != null && obj.method() // safe with &&
  obj != null & obj.method()  // NPE risk with &
examples.explanation: Short-circuit evaluation is a critical safety mechanism: obj != null && obj.value > 0 is safe because if obj is null, the second condition never executes. If you used &, both sides would execute and you'd get NPE. The non-short-circuit & and | are for boolean expressions where you explicitly need both sides evaluated (e.g., two method calls that both must run for their side effects).
examples.realWorldUse: Spring Security and JPA frequently use chained null-safe checks: `user != null && user.isEnabled() && user.getRole() != null && user.getRole().hasPermission(...)`. Each guard uses && to prevent NPE on the next check. In database queries, short-circuit prevents calling expensive methods when early conditions fail. Kotlin's ?. operator is syntactic sugar for this pattern.

examples.title: Ternary, instanceof, and Operator Precedence
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":4,"lineCode":"String label = (x > 5) ? \"big\" : \"small\";","variables":{"x":10,"label":"big"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"x>5 = 10>5 = true → return \"big\". Ternary evaluates to one of two values based on condition"},{"line":16,"lineCode":"if (obj instanceof String s)","variables":{"obj":"Hello","s":"Hello"},"phase":"CONDITION_TRUE","annotation":"Pattern matching instanceof (Java 16+): checks type AND binds to variable s. No explicit cast needed"},{"line":27,"lineCode":"System.out.println(2 + 3 * 4);","variables":{"result":14},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Precedence: * has higher precedence than +. 3*4=12 first, then 2+12=14"},{"line":29,"lineCode":"true || false && false","variables":{"result":true},"phase":"CONDITION_TRUE","annotation":"&& has higher precedence than ||. false&&false=false first. Then true||false=true"},{"line":32,"lineCode":"\"Value: \" + 1 + 2","variables":{"result":"Value: 12"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"String + is left-to-right: (\"Value: \"+1)=\"Value: 1\", then (\"Value: 1\"+2)=\"Value: 12\". Once String is in expression, + means concat"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    subgraph Operator Precedence High to Low
      P1[Postfix: x++, x--]
      P2[Prefix: ++x, -x, ~, !]
      P3[Multiplicative: * / %]
      P4[Additive: + -]
      P5[Shift: << >> >>>]
      P6[Relational: < > <= >= instanceof]
      P7[Equality: == !=]
      P8[Bitwise AND: &]
      P9[Bitwise XOR: ^]
      P10[Bitwise OR: |]
      P11[Logical AND: &&]
      P12[Logical OR: ||]
      P13[Ternary: ?:]
      P14[Assignment: = += -= ...]
    end
examples.pseudocode:
Ternary: cond ? a : b
  if cond: evaluate and return a
  else:    evaluate and return b
  Type: common type of a and b

instanceof pattern (Java 16+):
  if (obj instanceof Type varName) { /* varName ready to use */ }
  No explicit cast needed inside the block

Precedence traps:
  * before +     (always)
  && before ||   (logical AND before OR)
  + is left-associative (left to right)
  String + concatenates when either side is String
examples.explanation: Operator precedence governs evaluation order without parentheses. The && vs || precedence surprises many: `a || b && c` is `a || (b && c)` not `(a || b) && c`. The String + concatenation quirk: once a String appears in a + expression, subsequent + operations become concatenation, evaluated left-to-right. `1 + 2 + "x"` = `"3x"` but `"x" + 1 + 2` = `"x12"`. Pattern matching instanceof (Java 16+) eliminates a common verbosity in polymorphic code.
examples.realWorldUse: Pattern matching instanceof is used heavily in equals() implementations: `if (!(obj instanceof Person p)) return false; return this.name.equals(p.name);`. In Spring, ApplicationContext.getBean() results are often cast with instanceof checks. Ternary is preferred for simple conditional assignments in stream operations: `.map(x -> x > 0 ? "positive" : "non-positive")`.

problems.title: Check Power of Two
problems.description: Write a method `isPowerOfTwo(int n)` that returns true if n is a positive power of 2. Use the bitwise trick: a power of 2 has exactly one bit set, so n & (n-1) == 0.
problems.hint1: Powers of 2 in binary: 1=0001, 2=0010, 4=0100, 8=1000. Only one bit is set.
problems.hint2: n & (n-1) clears the lowest set bit. For power of 2: that clears the ONLY bit → result is 0.
problems.hint3: Also check n > 0 to exclude 0 and negative numbers.
problems.constraints: Must use bitwise operator n & (n-1). Must handle n <= 0. No loops allowed.
problems.editorial: A number is a power of two iff it has exactly one set bit: n > 0 && (n & (n-1)) == 0. The n-1 trick works because subtracting 1 flips the rightmost set bit and all lower bits. Powers of 2: 1, 2, 4, 8, 16... Edge case: 0 is not a power of 2. Long.bitCount(n) == 1 is an alternative.

problems.title: Count Set Bits (Popcount)
problems.description: Write a method `countBits(int n)` that counts the number of 1-bits in the binary representation of n (also called Hamming weight or popcount). Implement two versions: (1) simple loop, (2) Brian Kernighan's algorithm using n & (n-1).
problems.hint1: Simple: loop 32 times, check each bit with (n >> i) & 1.
problems.hint2: Kernighan: n & (n-1) removes the lowest set bit. Count how many times you can do this before n becomes 0.
problems.hint3: Java has Integer.bitCount(n) built-in — mention it but implement manually too.
problems.constraints: Must implement both approaches. Use >>> not >> in simple version for correct handling of negative numbers.
problems.editorial: Integer.bitCount(n) counts set bits in O(1). Manual approach: n & 1 extracts the LSB, then n >>>= 1 (unsigned right shift). Loop until n == 0. The Brian Kernighan trick: n &= (n-1) removes the lowest set bit each iteration — O(number of set bits) which is optimal.

problems.title: Swap Two Numbers Without Temp Variable
problems.description: Swap two integers using XOR without using a temporary variable. Explain why it works and demonstrate it. Also show the arithmetic swap (a = a+b; b = a-b; a = a-b) and why XOR is preferred.
problems.hint1: XOR swap: a ^= b; b ^= a; a ^= b. Property: a ^ b ^ b = a (XOR with same value twice cancels).
problems.hint2: Arithmetic swap has overflow risk: a+b might overflow int range.
problems.hint3: Warning: XOR swap fails when a and b refer to the same memory location (a == b references).
problems.constraints: Must NOT use a temporary variable. Must demonstrate the 3-step XOR swap. Mention the same-location pitfall.
problems.editorial: XOR swap: a ^= b; b ^= a; a ^= b; — works because XOR is its own inverse. a^b^b = a. Arithmetic swap: a = a+b; b = a-b; a = a-b; — risks integer overflow. Destructuring swap (Java 14+): var temp trick. XOR swap fails if a and b are aliases (same memory location).

problems.title: Ternary Chain: Grade Calculator
problems.description: Write a method `getGrade(int score)` using a ternary chain that returns letter grade: A (>=90), B (>=80), C (>=70), D (>=60), F (<60). Also write a switch expression version for comparison.
problems.hint1: Ternary chain: score >= 90 ? 'A' : score >= 80 ? 'B' : ... : 'F'
problems.hint2: For switch expression: use score / 10 to group: case 10, 9 -> 'A'; case 8 -> 'B'; etc.
problems.hint3: Ternary right-associative: a ? b : c ? d : e means a ? b : (c ? d : e).
problems.constraints: Must implement both ternary and switch expression versions. Results must be identical.
problems.editorial: Nested ternary: condition ? a : (condition2 ? b : c). Evaluate left to right. Use parentheses for clarity. Java ternary is right-associative. For letter grade: score>=90?'A':score>=80?'B':score>=70?'C':score>=60?'D':'F'. If-else chains are more readable for complex logic.

problems.title: Evaluate Short-Circuit Behavior
problems.description: Write a method that demonstrates short-circuit evaluation by counting how many times each operand is evaluated. Given expressions like `alwaysFalse() && expensive()`, show that expensive() is NOT called when alwaysFalse() returns false.
problems.hint1: Use counter variables incremented inside each method to track call counts.
problems.hint2: Test: false && X (X not called), true || X (X not called), false || X (X called), true && X (X called).
problems.hint3: Return a 2D int array: [leftCallCount, rightCallCount] for each expression.
problems.constraints: Must use separate call-count variables. Short-circuit expressions must show right=0 when appropriate.
problems.editorial: Short-circuit: && stops at first false operand. || stops at first true operand. Right side is not evaluated if left determines the result. Use this deliberately: if (list != null && !list.isEmpty()). The side effect test: increment a counter in the right operand — check if it was called.

problems.title: Bit Flag Permission System
problems.description: Implement a file permission system using bit flags. Define constants for READ(4), WRITE(2), EXECUTE(1). Write methods: `setPermission`, `clearPermission`, `hasPermission`, `getPermissionString` (returns rwx format like '755').
problems.hint1: SET: perms |= flag. CLEAR: perms &= ~flag. CHECK: (perms & flag) != 0.
problems.hint2: getPermissionString: check each bit and build string 'r' or '-', 'w' or '-', 'x' or '-'.
problems.hint3: For octal: READ=4, WRITE=2, EXECUTE=1. Each rwx group is one octal digit.
problems.constraints: Must use bitwise operations only (no if-else chains for set/clear). getPermissionString must use hasPermission.
problems.editorial: Use bit flags (powers of 2) for permissions: READ=1, WRITE=2, EXECUTE=4. Set: perms |= WRITE. Unset: perms &= ~WRITE. Check: (perms & READ) != 0. Combine: READ | WRITE = 3. This is the pattern behind Unix file permissions (rwxrwxrwx) and Java's java.nio.file.attribute.PosixFilePermission.

problems.title: Arithmetic Expression Evaluator with Precedence
problems.description: Write a method that evaluates a simple arithmetic expression string with correct operator precedence. The expression contains only: single-digit integers, +, -, *, /, parentheses, and spaces. Return the integer result.
problems.hint1: Use the recursive descent parser pattern: parseExpr → parseTerm → parseFactor.
problems.hint2: parseExpr handles + and - (low precedence). parseTerm handles * and / (high precedence). parseFactor handles numbers and ().
problems.hint3: Use an index[0] array to track position in the string while recursing.
problems.constraints: Must respect operator precedence (* before +). Must handle parentheses. Multi-digit numbers should work.
problems.editorial: Shunting-yard algorithm: use two stacks (operators, output). Handle precedence (* before +). Scan tokens left to right: numbers go to output, operators go to operator stack after popping higher/equal precedence ops. Parentheses: push '(', pop until '(' on ')'. Post-fix evaluate with a value stack.

problems.title: Reverse Bits of Integer
problems.description: Write a method `reverseBits(int n)` that reverses the bits of a 32-bit integer. Example: 00000000000000000000000000000001 (1) becomes 10000000000000000000000000000000 (-2147483648 or 2147483648 unsigned).
problems.hint1: Loop 32 times: extract lowest bit of n (n & 1), shift it to correct position in result (bit << (31 - i)).
problems.hint2: Alternative: result = (result << 1) | (n & 1); n >>>= 1; — build result bit by bit.
problems.hint3: Use unsigned right shift (>>>) to correctly handle the sign bit of n.
problems.constraints: Must use >>> (unsigned right shift) for correct bit extraction. Must process exactly 32 bits.
problems.editorial: Reverse bits by extracting LSB with n & 1, placing it at the MSB position of result, shifting result left, shifting n right. For 32 bits: loop 32 times. Alternative: use Integer.reverse(n) (JDK built-in). Be careful with the unsigned right shift >>> to avoid sign-extension.

problems.title: Single Number — XOR Find Unique
problems.description: Given an array where every number appears exactly twice except for one number which appears once, find that unique number. Use XOR: since a ^ a = 0 for any a, all paired numbers cancel out, leaving the unique one. O(n) time, O(1) space.
problems.hint1: XOR all elements together. Pairs cancel (a ^ a = 0), unique number remains (0 ^ x = x).
problems.hint2: Order doesn't matter: XOR is commutative and associative.
problems.hint3: For the extension: find two unique numbers when exactly two appear once — use the XOR of both and find a differentiating bit.
problems.constraints: Must use XOR (single pass, O(n) time, O(1) space). No HashMap or sorting allowed.
problems.editorial: XOR of a pair of equal numbers is 0. XOR of all numbers where every number appears twice except one: all pairs cancel out, leaving the unique number. x ^ x = 0, x ^ 0 = x, XOR is commutative and associative. O(n) time, O(1) space — optimal solution.

problems.title: Compute Without Arithmetic Operators
problems.description: Implement addition of two integers WITHOUT using + or - operators. Use only bitwise operators. Hint: in binary, a + b = (a XOR b) + carry, where carry = (a AND b) << 1. Apply recursively until carry is 0.
problems.hint1: Sum without carry: a ^ b (XOR). Carry: (a & b) << 1. Add sum and carry recursively.
problems.hint2: Base case: when carry == 0, return sum (a ^ b).
problems.hint3: In Java, be careful with 32-bit overflow in the carry shift. Use & 0xFFFFFFFFL or mask properly.
problems.constraints: Must NOT use +, -, *, / operators. Only bitwise operators allowed (^, &, |, ~, <<, >>).
problems.editorial: Addition with bit manipulation: sum = a ^ b (XOR gives sum without carry). carry = (a & b) << 1 (AND gives carry positions, shift left to add). Repeat until carry == 0. Handle negative numbers with two's complement (Java ints are already two's complement). Watch for infinite loop if both negative.

problems.title: Operator Overloading Simulation with BigDecimal
problems.description: Java doesn't support operator overloading. Write a Money class that wraps BigDecimal and provides add(), subtract(), multiply(int qty), and divide(int divisors) methods with proper financial rounding. Demonstrate how verbose it is compared to hypothetical operator overloading.
problems.hint1: Store amount as BigDecimal internally. Always use String constructor to avoid float precision issues.
problems.hint2: Use RoundingMode.HALF_UP for all divisions. Scale to 2 decimal places for currency.
problems.hint3: Make the class immutable: all operations return new Money instances.
problems.constraints: Must use BigDecimal(String) constructor. Must set scale=2. All operations return new Money (immutable). Use HALF_UP rounding.
problems.editorial: BigDecimal has no operator overloading in Java. Wrap BigDecimal in a class with add(Money other), multiply(int factor) etc. returning new instances. Use compareTo() not equals() for value comparison (equals() also compares scale). This is the correct pattern for money arithmetic in Java.

problems.title: Modular Arithmetic Ring
problems.description: Implement a `ModInt` class representing integers modulo a given N. Operations (+, -, *, pow) should all produce results in [0, N). Handle negative modulo correctly (Java's % can return negative).
problems.hint1: Always-positive modulo: ((n % m) + m) % m.
problems.hint2: For pow(base, exp, mod): use fast exponentiation: if exp is even, compute half then square.
problems.hint3: For subtraction: (a - b + mod) % mod — add mod before taking remainder to prevent negative result.
problems.constraints: Constructor must normalize to [0, mod) range. Use ((v%m)+m)%m. multiply must use long to prevent overflow. pow must use fast exponentiation.
problems.editorial: Modular arithmetic: all operations mod m. Addition: (a + b) % m. Multiplication: (a * b) % m. Use long intermediate to prevent overflow when a and b are up to int max. Negative modulo in Java: (-5 % 3) == -2, not 1 — fix with ((n % m) + m) % m for always-positive results.

problems.title: Bitmasked Sudoku Validity Checker
problems.description: Use bit manipulation to efficiently check if a Sudoku board is valid. For each row, column, and 3x3 box, maintain a bitmask of seen digits (1-9). Using bit 1 for digit 1, bit 2 for digit 2, etc. Check for duplicates with the & operator.
problems.hint1: Use bitmask: int seen = 0. For digit d, bit d should be set. Check: (seen & (1 << d)) != 0 means duplicate.
problems.hint2: Maintain three arrays of bitmasks: rows[9], cols[9], boxes[9]. For cell (r,c), box index = (r/3)*3 + (c/3).
problems.hint3: Set the bit: seen |= (1 << d). On conflict, the bit is already set.

testCases.input: invalid board with duplicate in column

problems.constraints: Must use bitmasks for row/col/box tracking. bit = 1 << digit. Check before set. O(81) time O(27) space.
problems.editorial: Represent each row/column/3x3 box as a bitmask. For each cell value v, check if bit (1 << v) is already set in the mask — if so, duplicate found. Set the bit. Use 27 masks total (9 rows + 9 cols + 9 boxes). O(81) time, O(27) space — the most efficient Sudoku validator approach.

problems.title: Implement Multiplication Using Bit Shifts
problems.description: Implement `multiply(int a, int b)` using only bit shifts and addition (no * operator). Use the standard binary multiplication algorithm: for each set bit in b, add a shifted left by the bit position.
problems.hint1: Binary multiplication: 5 * 6 = 5 * (4 + 2) = 5*4 + 5*2 = 20 + 10 = 30. Each set bit in b contributes a * (1 << bitPos).
problems.hint2: Loop through each bit of b (0-31). If bit i is set in b, add (a << i) to result.
problems.hint3: Handle negative numbers: work with absolute values, then negate if one operand is negative.
problems.constraints: Must NOT use * operator. Use bit shifts and addition only. Handle negative numbers correctly.
problems.editorial: Russian peasant multiplication using bit shifts: result += (a << bit) when the corresponding bit of b is set. Or iteratively: while b > 0: if b is odd, result += a; a <<= 1; b >>= 1. This is O(log n) and how hardware multipliers worked before dedicated multiply circuits.

problems.title: Operator Precedence Trap Quiz
problems.description: Create a class with 10 tricky Java operator precedence quiz questions. Each question shows an expression and four choices. Return a List<String> of the correct answers and explanations.
problems.hint1: Traps: && before ||, & before &&, String + is left-associative, ternary is right-associative.
problems.hint2: Include: int vs double division, pre vs post increment in expressions, String + number ordering.
problems.hint3: Return as list of 'expression = result: explanation' strings.
problems.constraints: Must include exactly 10 questions. Must cover: *, &&/||, char+int, String+int, prefix/postfix, integer division, >>> , ternary associativity, byte overflow.
problems.editorial: Operator precedence traps: (1) ++ prefix vs postfix — x = i++ vs x = ++i; (2) bitwise & before &&; (3) ternary lower precedence than arithmetic; (4) = (assignment) very low precedence — if (x = getValue()) != 0 needs parentheses; (5) >>> vs >> — unsigned vs signed shift.

problems.title: Hamming Distance Calculator
problems.description: The Hamming distance between two integers is the number of bit positions where they differ. Implement `hammingDistance(int x, int y)` and `totalHammingDistance(int[] nums)` — the sum of Hamming distances between all pairs.
problems.hint1: Hamming distance = Integer.bitCount(x ^ y). XOR gives 1 in positions where bits differ.
problems.hint2: For totalHammingDistance: brute force is O(n²). Optimized: for each bit position, count how many numbers have it set (k). Pairs with different bits = k * (n-k).
problems.hint3: Iterate 32 bit positions. For position i, count set bits: c = how many nums have bit i set. Contribution = c * (nums.length - c).
problems.constraints: hammingDistance must use Integer.bitCount(x^y). totalHammingDistance must be O(32*n), not O(n²).
problems.editorial: Hamming distance: XOR the two numbers (bits differ where XOR is 1), then count set bits with Integer.bitCount(). x = a ^ b; return Integer.bitCount(x). O(1) time. The bitCount algorithm uses Brian Kernighan's trick or SWAR (SIMD Within A Register) population count.

problems.title: Gray Code Generator
problems.description: Generate an n-bit Gray code sequence where consecutive values differ by exactly one bit. The formula is: gray(i) = i ^ (i >> 1). Generate the first 2^n values and verify each consecutive pair differs by exactly 1 bit.
problems.hint1: Gray code formula: gray(i) = i XOR (i right-shift 1). This produces the standard binary-reflected Gray code.
problems.hint2: Verify: for each consecutive pair g[i] and g[i+1], Integer.bitCount(g[i] ^ g[i+1]) should equal 1.
problems.hint3: For the reverse: given Gray code g, binary = g ^ (g>>1) ^ (g>>2) ^ ... until shift >= bits.
problems.constraints: Must use i ^ (i >> 1) formula. Must verify each consecutive pair differs by exactly 1 bit. Implement gray-to-binary conversion.
problems.editorial: Gray code sequence: 0,1,3,2,6,7,5,4... Formula: gray(n) = n ^ (n >> 1). To generate sequence: for i in 0..2^n-1, output i^(i>>1). Property: adjacent codes differ by exactly 1 bit. Inverse: decode by XOR prefix scan. Used in rotary encoders to eliminate invalid states during transitions.

problems.title: Maximum AND Pair
problems.description: Given an array of integers, find the pair (a, b) where a AND b is maximized. Brute force is O(n²). Optimized: try to build the maximum result bit by bit from the most significant bit, keeping only numbers that have that bit set.
problems.hint1: Brute force: for all pairs check a & b and track maximum.
problems.hint2: Optimized (O(30*n)): try to set each bit of result from MSB to LSB. Tentatively set the bit, keep candidates with that bit set. If >= 2 candidates remain, the bit can be in the answer.
problems.hint3: Start with ans=0, candidates=all numbers. For each bit from 29 down to 0: filter candidates keeping only those with both bit set in candidate AND (ans | 1<<bit).
problems.constraints: Both brute and optimized must give same answer. Optimized must work in O(30*n) using greedy bit-by-bit approach.
problems.editorial: For maximum AND pair: greedily set bits from MSB. For each bit from 31 to 0: check if at least 2 numbers have this bit set (using count of numbers with mask & bit). If yes, include in answer mask. O(32n) = O(n) time. The AND of two numbers cannot exceed either operand.

problems.title: Decode Ways Using Bitmask DP
problems.description: A string of digits can be decoded where 'A'=1, 'B'=2, ..., 'Z'=26. Count the number of ways to decode a given digit string. Use a bitmask approach or dynamic programming with bitwise operations to track valid decode states.
problems.hint1: DP approach: dp[i] = number of ways to decode s[0..i-1]. dp[i] += dp[i-1] if s[i-1] is non-zero, dp[i] += dp[i-2] if s[i-2..i-1] forms 10-26.
problems.hint2: Use bitwise checks: s.charAt(i) != '0' is single-digit valid. Valid two-digit: (s[i-2]=='1') || (s[i-2]=='2' && s[i-1]<='6').
problems.hint3: Space-optimized: only need previous two values — use two variables instead of array.
problems.constraints: O(n) time, O(1) space DP using two rolling variables. Handle leading zero. Handle '0' digits (only valid as second digit of 10 or 20).
problems.editorial: Bitmask DP: dp[mask] = number of ways to decode the subset represented by mask. Transition: try extending current decode with 1 or 2 digits. dp[0] = 1 (empty string). For each position, check single digit (1-9) and double digit (10-26). The bitmask tracks which characters have been consumed.

problems.title: Bitwise Puzzle: Find Missing and Duplicate in Array
problems.description:
Given an array of n integers where elements should be from 1 to n, but one element is missing and one is duplicated — find both using O(n) time and O(1) space using only bitwise XOR and arithmetic operators.

Example: [1,2,2,4] → missing=3, duplicate=2
problems.hint1: XOR of 1..n XOR all elements = missing ^ duplicate (since all others cancel).
problems.hint2: We need another equation: use sum. Sum(1..n) - sum(array) = missing - duplicate.
problems.hint3: Two equations: xorVal = missing ^ duplicate, diff = missing - duplicate. Find a distinguishing bit in xorVal, then split elements and 1..n into two groups, XOR each group.
problems.constraints: O(n) time, O(1) space. Must use XOR. Two-pass: first XOR pass, then split-by-bit pass. Final check to determine which is missing vs duplicate.
problems.editorial: XOR find missing and duplicate in array [1..n]: XOR all elements with all of 1..n. Result = missing ^ duplicate. Then use sum formula: sum(array) - sum(1..n) = duplicate - missing. Solve the two equations. O(n) time, O(1) space. No sorting, no extra storage.
